{{short description|Neuroscientist}}

| known_for         = Study of [[memory]] and [[trauma (medicine)|trauma]]
}}

'''Daniela Schiller''' ([[Hebrew language|Hebrew]]: דניאלה שילר; born October 26, 1972, in Israel<ref name="Yogis2013">{{cite book|last=Yogis|first=Jaimal|title=The Fear Project: What Our Most Primal Emotion Taught Me About Survival, Success, Surfing . . . and Love|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=qNM6MGL9bSgC&pg=PA18|access-date=10 July 2013|date=2013-01-08|publisher=Rodale|isbn=978-1-60961-176-7|pages=18–20}}</ref>) is a [[neuroscientist]] who leads the [[Affective neuroscience|Affective Neuroscience]] Lab at the [[Mount Sinai School of Medicine]].<ref>{{cite web|url=http://neuroscience.mssm.edu/schiller/|title=Neuroscience Department - Schiller Lab Home|publisher=Mount Sinai School of Medicine|access-date=10 July 2013|archive-date=6 November 2013|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20131106011711/http://neuroscience.mssm.edu/schiller/}}{{psc|date=July 2020}}</ref> She is best known for her work on [[memory reconsolidation]], and on modification of emotional learning and memory.<ref name=mittr>{{cite news|url=http://www.technologyreview.com/featuredstory/515981/repairing-bad-memories/|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130619042930/http://www.technologyreview.com/featuredstory/515981/repairing-bad-memories/|archive-date=June 19, 2013|title=Neuroscientist Daniela Schiller is Researching Ways that Bad Memories Can be Made Less Fearsome|last=Hall|first=Stephen S.|date=June 17, 2013|work=MIT Technology Review|access-date=10 July 2013}}</ref><ref>{{cite news|url=https://www.telegraph.co.uk/science/science-news/6769336/Trauma-and-fear-to-be-erased-from-your-mind.html|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20091212231011/http://www.telegraph.co.uk/science/science-news/6769336/Trauma-and-fear-to-be-erased-from-your-mind.html|archive-date=December 12, 2009|title=Trauma and fear to be erased from your mind|last=Alleyne|first=Richard|date=December 10, 2009|work=[[The Daily Telegraph]]|access-date=10 July 2013}}</ref><ref>{{cite news|url=https://www.newscientist.com/article/dn21701-drugfree-therapy-makes-addicts-forget-addiction.html|title=Drug-free therapy makes addicts 'forget' addiction|last=Reardon|first=Sara|date=April 13, 2012|work=[[New Scientist]]|access-date=10 July 2013}}</ref>

==Early life and education==
Schiller was born in [[Rishon LeZion, Israel]]. She is the daughter of a [[Moroccan people|Moroccan]] mother and a [[Ukrainians|Ukrainian]] father. Schiller's father, Sigmund Schiller, is a survivor of the [[Holocaust]]. Schiller is the youngest of four children. She received a bachelor's degree in [[psychology]] and [[philosophy]] in 1996, and her doctorate in [[psychobiology]] from [[Tel Aviv University]] in 2004. She was awarded a [[Fulbright]] fellowship and worked with [[Elizabeth A. Phelps]] and [[Joseph E. LeDoux]] at [[New York University]].<ref name="New Yorker">{{cite news|last=Specter|first=Michael|title=Partial Recall|newspaper=The New Yorker|date=19 May 2014|url=http://www.newyorker.com/magazine/2014/05/19/partial-recall|access-date=27 February 2015}}</ref> Schiller plays drums and sings backing vocals for [[The Amygdaloids]]<ref name=mittr/><ref>{{cite news|url=https://www.nytimes.com/2007/03/06/nyregion/06ink.html|title=INK; A Band of Scientists Who Really Are a Band|last=Heydarpour|first=Roja|date=6 March 2007|work=The New York Times|page=2|access-date=10 July 2013}}</ref> and Supersmall.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://supersmallmusic.com/|title=Supersmall – Silent Moon|access-date=2020-08-07|archive-date=2020-08-06|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200806214856/https://supersmallmusic.com/}}</ref>

== Awards and recognition==
* 2014 Klingenstein-Simons Fellowship in Neuroscience<ref>{{Cite web|title=The Esther A. & and Joseph Klingenstein Fund, Inc.|url=https://www.klingfund.org/fellows/fellows17.php|access-date=2020-06-10|website=www.klingfund.org|archive-date=2020-08-07|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200807114204/https://www.klingfund.org/fellows/fellows17.php}}</ref>
* 2013 Kavli Frontiers of Science Fellow, National Academy of Sciences<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.nasonline.org/programs/kavli-frontiers-of-science/|title=Kavli Frontiers Home|website=www.nasonline.org}}</ref>
* 2010 [[Blavatnik Award for Young Scientists]]<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://blavatnikawards.org/honorees/profile/daniela-schiller/|title=Daniela Schiller &#124; Blavatnik Awards for Young Scientists|website=blavatnikawards.org}}</ref>
* 2005 Fulbright Scholar<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.fulbright.org.il/program/1/35|title=Fulbright Postdoctoral Fellowship &#124; Fulbright|website=www.fulbright.org.il|access-date=2020-07-12|archive-date=2020-08-06|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200806163246/https://www.fulbright.org.il/program/1/35}}</ref>

==Scientific research==
The goal of Schiller's research is to unravel the neurocognitive mechanisms that make emotional memories malleable, allowing for memory modification and for the adaptive adjustment of emotional and social behavior.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://labs.neuroscience.mssm.edu/project/schiller-lab/|title=Schiller Lab &#124; Neuroscience Labs - Icahn School of Medicine|website=labs.neuroscience.mssm.edu|access-date=2020-07-12|archive-date=2022-12-06|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20221206042349/http://labs.neuroscience.mssm.edu/project/schiller-lab/}}</ref>

===Research on the modulation of fear learning===
Schiller's research addressed this question by using a behavioral paradigm called reversal learning in conjunction with physiological skin conductance measurements and neuroimaging. In this task, subjects first learned to associate one of two neutral stimuli with an aversive outcome (acquisition stage), and then had to flexibly modify this learning when the second stimulus began to predict the aversive outcome, while the initial predictive stimulus ceased to do so (reversal stage). The study found that responses in the amygdala and the [[striatum]] flexibly tracked the predictive aversive value of the conditioned stimuli, and switched their responses from one stimulus to another when reversal occurred. The [[ventromedial prefrontal cortex]] (vmPFC) also participated albeit in the opposite direction, showing stronger responses to the safe stimuli, but also dissociating 'naïve' safe stimuli from stimuli that used to be dangerous but are now safe.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Schiller |first1=D. |last2=Levy |first2=I. |last3=Niv |first3=Y. |last4=LeDoux |first4=J. E. |last5=Phelps |first5=E. A. |title=From Fear to Safety and Back: Reversal of Fear in the Human Brain |journal=Journal of Neuroscience |date=5 November 2008 |volume=28 |issue=45 |pages=11517–11525 |doi=10.1523/JNEUROSCI.2265-08.2008 |pmid=18987188 |pmc=3844784 |doi-access=free }}{{psc|date=July 2020}}</ref> In order to identify a general mechanism underlying fear modulation regardless of the particular strategy used, Schiller and Mauricio Delgado demonstrated the overlapping neural systems mediating extinction, reversal and regulation of learned fear.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Schiller |first1=Daniela |last2=Delgado |first2=Mauricio R. |title=Overlapping neural systems mediating extinction, reversal and regulation of fear |journal=Trends in Cognitive Sciences |date=June 2010 |volume=14 |issue=6 |pages=268–276 |doi=10.1016/j.tics.2010.04.002 |pmid=20493762 |pmc=3848321 }}{{psc|date=July 2020}}</ref> Further research used the reversal learning data to dissociate the different computations performed by the striatum (prediction error) and amygdala (associability) during fear learning.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Li |first1=Jian |last2=Schiller |first2=Daniela |last3=Schoenbaum |first3=Geoffrey |last4=Phelps |first4=Elizabeth A |last5=Daw |first5=Nathaniel D |title=Differential roles of human striatum and amygdala in associative learning |journal=Nature Neuroscience |date=11 September 2011 |volume=14 |issue=10 |pages=1250–1252 |doi=10.1038/nn.2904 |pmid=21909088 |pmc=3268261 }}{{psc|date=July 2020}}</ref> The reversal protocol also helped identifying differences between combat veterans with or without a PTSD diagnosis in how they compute prediction error and update the value of fear predictive stimuli, and the neural tracking of these computations.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Homan |first1=Philipp |last2=Levy |first2=Ifat |last3=Feltham |first3=Eric |last4=Gordon |first4=Charles |last5=Hu |first5=Jingchu |last6=Li |first6=Jian |last7=Pietrzak |first7=Robert H. |last8=Southwick |first8=Steven |last9=Krystal |first9=John H. |last10=Harpaz-Rotem |first10=Ilan |last11=Schiller |first11=Daniela |title=Neural computations of threat in the aftermath of combat trauma |journal=Nature Neuroscience |date=21 January 2019 |volume=22 |issue=3 |pages=470–476 |doi=10.1038/s41593-018-0315-x |pmid=30664770 |pmc=6829910 }}{{psc|date=July 2020}}</ref> Schiller's investigation was extended also to instrumental learning of active avoidance, revealing the neural mechanisms that predict successful active coping with threats in the human brain.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Collins |first1=K. A. |last2=Mendelsohn |first2=A. |last3=Cain |first3=C. K. |last4=Schiller |first4=D. |title=Taking Action in the Face of Threat: Neural Synchronization Predicts Adaptive Coping |journal=Journal of Neuroscience |date=29 October 2014 |volume=34 |issue=44 |pages=14733–14738 |doi=10.1523/JNEUROSCI.2152-14.2014 |pmid=25355225 |pmc=4212070 |doi-access=free }}{{psc|date=July 2020}}</ref>

===Research on memory reconsolidation===
To examine the ability to modify emotional memory, Schiller's research focused on reconsolidation, which is a memory process of restabilizing a destabilized memory.<ref name=pmid27885549>{{cite book |doi=10.1007/7854_2016_463 |chapter=Memory Reconsolidation |title=Behavioral Neuroscience of Learning and Memory |series=Current Topics in Behavioral Neurosciences |year=2016 |last1=Haubrich |first1=Josue |last2=Nader |first2=Karim |volume=37 |pages=151–176 |pmid=27885549 |isbn=978-3-319-78755-8 }}</ref> Reconsolidation can be blocked using pharmacological agents,<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Nader |first1=Karim |last2=Schafe |first2=Glenn E. |last3=Le Doux |first3=Joseph E. |s2cid=4420637 |title=Fear memories require protein synthesis in the amygdala for reconsolidation after retrieval |journal=Nature |date=17 August 2000 |volume=406 |issue=6797 |pages=722–726 |doi=10.1038/35021052 |pmid=10963596 |bibcode=2000Natur.406..722N }}</ref> or non-invasive behavioral interference such as new motor learning during the reconsolidation of motor memories,<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Walker |first1=Matthew P. |last2=Brakefield |first2=Tiffany |last3=Allan Hobson |first3=J. |last4=Stickgold |first4=Robert |s2cid=4431941 |title=Dissociable stages of human memory consolidation and reconsolidation |journal=Nature |date=October 2003 |volume=425 |issue=6958 |pages=616–620 |doi=10.1038/nature01930 |pmid=14534587 |bibcode=2003Natur.425..616W }}</ref> new episodic learning during reconsolidation of declarative memory,<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Hupbach |first1=A. |last2=Gomez |first2=R. |last3=Hardt |first3=O. |last4=Nadel |first4=L. |title=Reconsolidation of episodic memories: A subtle reminder triggers integration of new information |journal=Learning & Memory |date=3 January 2007 |volume=14 |issue=1–2 |pages=47–53 |doi=10.1101/lm.365707 |pmid=17202429 |pmc=1838545 }}</ref> and extinction learning during the reconsolidation of fear memory.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Monfils |first1=M.-H. |last2=Cowansage |first2=K. K. |last3=Klann |first3=E. |last4=LeDoux |first4=J. E. |title=Extinction-Reconsolidation Boundaries: Key to Persistent Attenuation of Fear Memories |journal=Science |date=1 April 2009 |volume=324 |issue=5929 |pages=951–955 |doi=10.1126/science.1167975 |pmid=19342552 |pmc=3625935 |bibcode=2009Sci...324..951M }}</ref> Schiller's research demonstrated the interference of reconsolidation of fear memory using extinction in humans.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Schiller |first1=Daniela |last2=Monfils |first2=Marie-H. |last3=Raio |first3=Candace M. |last4=Johnson |first4=David C. |last5=LeDoux |first5=Joseph E. |last6=Phelps |first6=Elizabeth A. |title=Preventing the return of fear in humans using reconsolidation update mechanisms |journal=Nature |date=9 December 2009 |volume=463 |issue=7277 |pages=49–53 |doi=10.1038/nature08637 |pmid=20010606 |pmc=3640262 }}{{psc|date=July 2020}}</ref> One failure to reproduce this latter finding in an independent study<ref>{{Cite journal|date=2020-08-01|title=No persistent attenuation of fear memories in humans: A registered replication of the reactivation-extinction effect|url= |journal=Cortex|language=en|volume=129|pages=496–509|doi=10.1016/j.cortex.2020.04.017|issn=0010-9452|pmc=7115861|pmid=32580869|last1=Chalkia|first1=Anastasia|last2=Schroyens|first2=Natalie|last3=Leng|first3=Lu|last4=Vanhasbroeck|first4=Niels|last5=Zenses|first5=Ann-Kathrin|last6=Van Oudenhove|first6=Lukas|last7=Beckers|first7=Tom}}</ref> or to validate the article's claims using the original data<ref>{{Cite journal|date=2020-08-01|title=Preventing the return of fear in humans using reconsolidation update mechanisms: A verification report of Schiller et al. (2010)|url= |journal=Cortex|language=en|volume=129|pages=510–525|doi=10.1016/j.cortex.2020.03.031|issn=0010-9452|pmc=7115860|pmid=32563517|last1=Chalkia|first1=Anastasia|last2=Van Oudenhove|first2=Lukas|last3=Beckers|first3=Tom}}</ref> have cast doubts on whether it can be replicated.<ref>{{Cite journal|date=2020-08-01|title=The three R's of scientific integrity: Replicability, reproducibility, and robustness|journal=Cortex|language=en|volume=129|pages=A4–A7|doi=10.1016/j.cortex.2020.04.019|issn=0010-9452|last1=McIntosh|first1=Robert D.|last2=Chambers|first2=Christopher D.|pmid=32563515|s2cid=219943250|doi-access=free}}</ref> However, the authors contend that it is valid,<ref name="jn6uw"/> the original data is publicly available and replicates,<ref name=jn6uw/> and of subsequent replications, about 80% (~50 experiments) were successful.<ref name=jn6uw>{{Cite journal|title=Reply to Beckers, McIntosh and Chambers on the verification of 'preventing the return of fear using retrieval-extinction in humans'|url=https://psyarxiv.com/jn6uw/|access-date=2021-04-18|website=psyarxiv.com|doi=10.31234/osf.io/jn6uw|last1=Schiller|first1=Daniela|last2=Ledoux|first2=Joseph E.|last3=Phelps|first3=Elizabeth A.|s2cid=236798278}}</ref>

Additional research demonstrated retrieval-extinction interference in mice,<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Rao-Ruiz |first1=Priyanka |last2=Rotaru |first2=Diana C |last3=van der Loo |first3=Rolinka J |last4=Mansvelder |first4=Huibert D |last5=Stiedl |first5=Oliver |last6=Smit |first6=August B |last7=Spijker |first7=Sabine |s2cid=7756062 |title=Retrieval-specific endocytosis of GluA2-AMPARs underlies adaptive reconsolidation of contextual fear |journal=Nature Neuroscience |date=11 September 2011 |volume=14 |issue=10 |pages=1302–1308 |doi=10.1038/nn.2907 |pmid=21909089 |url=https://hal.archives-ouvertes.fr/hal-00677961/file/PEER_stage2_10.1038%252Fnn.2907.pdf }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |last1=Escosteguy-Neto |first1=Joao Carlos |last2=Varela |first2=Patricia |last3=Correa-Neto |first3=Nelson Francisco |last4=Coelho |first4=Laura Segismundo |last5=Onaivi |first5=Emmanuel S. |last6=Santos-Junior |first6=Jair Guilherme |s2cid=41113005 |title=Reconsolidation and update of morphine-associated contextual memory in mice |journal=Neurobiology of Learning and Memory |date=April 2016 |volume=130 |pages=194–201 |doi=10.1016/j.nlm.2016.02.015 |pmid=26948121 }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |last1=Clem |first1=R. L. |last2=Huganir |first2=R. L. |title=Calcium-Permeable AMPA Receptor Dynamics Mediate Fear Memory Erasure |journal=Science |date=28 October 2010 |volume=330 |issue=6007 |pages=1108–1112 |doi=10.1126/science.1195298 |pmid=21030604 |pmc=3001394 |bibcode=2010Sci...330.1108C }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |last1=Gräff |first1=Johannes |last2=Joseph |first2=Nadine F. |last3=Horn |first3=Meryl E. |last4=Samiei |first4=Alireza |last5=Meng |first5=Jia |last6=Seo |first6=Jinsoo |last7=Rei |first7=Damien |last8=Bero |first8=Adam W. |last9=Phan |first9=Trongha X. |last10=Wagner |first10=Florence |last11=Holson |first11=Edward |last12=Xu |first12=Jinbin |last13=Sun |first13=Jianjun |last14=Neve |first14=Rachael L. |last15=Mach |first15=Robert H. |last16=Haggarty |first16=Stephen J. |last17=Tsai |first17=Li-Huei |title=Epigenetic Priming of Memory Updating during Reconsolidation to Attenuate Remote Fear Memories |journal=Cell |date=January 2014 |volume=156 |issue=1–2 |pages=261–276 |doi=10.1016/j.cell.2013.12.020 |pmid=24439381 |pmc=3986862 }}</ref> rats,<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Cofresí |first1=Roberto U. |last2=Lewis |first2=Suzanne M. |last3=Chaudhri |first3=Nadia |last4=Lee |first4=Hongjoo J. |last5=Monfils |first5=Marie-H. |last6=Gonzales |first6=Rueben A. |title=Postretrieval Extinction Attenuates Alcohol Cue Reactivity in Rats |journal=Alcoholism: Clinical and Experimental Research |date=March 2017 |volume=41 |issue=3 |pages=608–617 |doi=10.1111/acer.13323 |pmid=28169439 |pmc=5332343 }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |last1=Lee |first1=Hongjoo J. |last2=Haberman |first2=Rebecca P. |last3=Roquet |first3=Rheall F. |last4=Monfils |first4=Marie-H. |title=Extinction and Retrieval + Extinction of Conditioned Fear Differentially Activate Medial Prefrontal Cortex and Amygdala in Rats |journal=Frontiers in Behavioral Neuroscience |date=22 January 2016 |volume=9 |page=369 |doi=10.3389/fnbeh.2015.00369 |pmid=26834596 |pmc=4722140 |doi-access=free }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |last1=Tedesco |first1=Vincenzo |last2=Roquet |first2=Rheall F. |last3=DeMis |first3=John |last4=Chiamulera |first4=Cristiano |last5=Monfils |first5=Marie-H. |title=Extinction, applied after retrieval of auditory fear memory, selectively increases zinc-finger protein 268 and phosphorylated ribosomal protein S6 expression in prefrontal cortex and lateral amygdala |journal=Neurobiology of Learning and Memory |date=November 2014 |volume=115 |pages=78–85 |doi=10.1016/j.nlm.2014.08.015 |pmid=25196703 |doi-access=free }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |last1=Auchter |first1=Allison |last2=Cormack |first2=Lawrence K. |last3=Niv |first3=Yael |last4=Gonzalez-Lima |first4=Francisco |last5=Monfils |first5=Marie H. |title=Reconsolidation-Extinction Interactions in Fear Memory Attenuation: The Role of Inter-Trial Interval Variability |journal=Frontiers in Behavioral Neuroscience |date=24 January 2017 |volume=11 |page=2 |doi=10.3389/fnbeh.2017.00002 |pmid=28174526 |pmc=5258753 |doi-access=free }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |last1=Auchter |first1=Allison M. |last2=Shumake |first2=Jason |last3=Gonzalez-Lima |first3=Francisco |last4=Monfils |first4=Marie H. |title=Preventing the return of fear using reconsolidation updating and methylene blue is differentially dependent on extinction learning |journal=Scientific Reports |date=11 April 2017 |volume=7 |issue=1 |article-number=46071 |doi=10.1038/srep46071 |pmid=28397861 |pmc=5387397 |bibcode=2017NatSR...746071A |doi-access=free }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |last1=Flavell |first1=Charlotte R. |last2=Barber |first2=David J. |last3=Lee |first3=Jonathan L.C. |title=Behavioural memory reconsolidation of food and fear memories |journal=Nature Communications |date=18 October 2011 |volume=2 |issue=1 |page=504 |doi=10.1038/ncomms1515 |pmid=22009036 |pmc=3516828 |bibcode=2011NatCo...2..504F |doi-access=free }}</ref><ref name="pmid22499948">{{cite journal |last1=Xue |first1=Y.-X. |last2=Luo |first2=Y.-X. |last3=Wu |first3=P. |last4=Shi |first4=H.-S. |last5=Xue |first5=L.-F. |last6=Chen |first6=C. |last7=Zhu |first7=W.-L. |last8=Ding |first8=Z.-B. |last9=Bao |first9=Y.-p. |last10=Shi |first10=J. |last11=Epstein |first11=D. H. |last12=Shaham |first12=Y. |last13=Lu |first13=L. |title=A Memory Retrieval-Extinction Procedure to Prevent Drug Craving and Relapse |journal=Science |date=12 April 2012 |volume=336 |issue=6078 |pages=241–245 |doi=10.1126/science.1215070 |pmid=22499948 |pmc=3695463 |bibcode=2012Sci...336..241X }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |last1=Ferrer Monti |first1=Roque I. |last2=Alfei |first2=Joaquín M. |last3=Mugnaini |first3=Matías |last4=Bueno |first4=Adrián M. |last5=Beckers |first5=Tom |last6=Urcelay |first6=Gonzalo P. |last7=Molina |first7=Victor A. |title=A comparison of behavioral and pharmacological interventions to attenuate reactivated fear memories |journal=Learning & Memory |date=17 July 2017 |volume=24 |issue=8 |pages=369–374 |doi=10.1101/lm.045385.117 |pmid=28716956 |pmc=5516684 }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |last1=Olshavsky |first1=Megan E. |last2=Song |first2=Bryan J. |last3=Powell |first3=Daniel J. |last4=Jones |first4=Carolyn E. |last5=Monfils |first5=Marie-H. |last6=Lee |first6=Hongjoo J. |title=Updating appetitive memory during reconsolidation window: critical role of cue-directed behavior and amygdala central nucleus |journal=Frontiers in Behavioral Neuroscience |date=2013 |volume=7 |page=186 |doi=10.3389/fnbeh.2013.00186 |pmid=24367304 |pmc=3856395 |doi-access=free }}</ref><ref name="auto1">{{cite journal |last1=Liu |first1=Jianfeng |last2=Zhao |first2=Liyan |last3=Xue |first3=Yanxue |last4=Shi |first4=Jie |last5=Suo |first5=Lin |last6=Luo |first6=Yixiao |last7=Chai |first7=Baisheng |last8=Yang |first8=Chang |last9=Fang |first9=Qin |last10=Zhang |first10=Yan |last11=Bao |first11=Yanping |last12=Pickens |first12=Charles L. |last13=Lu |first13=Lin |title=An Unconditioned Stimulus Retrieval Extinction Procedure to Prevent the Return of Fear Memory |journal=Biological Psychiatry |date=December 2014 |volume=76 |issue=11 |pages=895–901 |doi=10.1016/j.biopsych.2014.03.027 |pmid=24813334 |pmc=4480632 }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |last1=Luo |first1=Yi-xiao |last2=Xue |first2=Yan-xue |last3=Liu |first3=Jian-feng |last4=Shi |first4=Hai-shui |last5=Jian |first5=Min |last6=Han |first6=Ying |last7=Zhu |first7=Wei-li |last8=Bao |first8=Yan-ping |last9=Wu |first9=Ping |last10=Ding |first10=Zeng-bo |last11=Shen |first11=Hao-wei |last12=Shi |first12=Jie |last13=Shaham |first13=Yavin |last14=Lu |first14=Lin |title=A novel UCS memory retrieval-extinction procedure to inhibit relapse to drug seeking |journal=Nature Communications |date=14 July 2015 |volume=6 |issue=1 |page=7675 |doi=10.1038/ncomms8675 |pmid=26169171 |pmc=4510700 |bibcode=2015NatCo...6.7675L }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |last1=Ponnusamy |first1=Ravikumar |last2=Zhuravka |first2=Irina |last3=Poulos |first3=Andrew M. |last4=Shobe |first4=Justin |last5=Merjanian |first5=Michael |last6=Huang |first6=Jeannie |last7=Wolvek |first7=David |last8=O'Neill |first8=Pia-Kelsey |last9=Fanselow |first9=Michael S. |title=Retrieval and Reconsolidation Accounts of Fear Extinction |journal=Frontiers in Behavioral Neuroscience |date=9 May 2016 |volume=10 |page=89 |doi=10.3389/fnbeh.2016.00089 |pmid=27242459 |pmc=4860411 |doi-access=free }}</ref> and humans.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Yang |first1=Yong |last2=Jie |first2=Jing |last3=Li |first3=Junjiao |last4=Chen |first4=Wei |last5=Zheng |first5=Xifu |title=A novel method to trigger the retrieval-extinction were shown in juvenile rats<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Jones |first1=Carolyn E. |last2=Monfils |first2=Marie-H. |title=Post-retrieval extinction in adolescence prevents return of humans.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Johnson |first1=D. |first1=Armita |last2=Tjaden |first2=Cathelijn |last3=Kindt |first3=Merel |title=Vicarious extinction learning during reconsolidation neutralizes fear memory |journal=Behaviour Research and Therapy |date=May 2017 |volume=92 |pages=87–93 |doi=10.1016/j.brat.2017.02.004 |pmid=28286265 }}</ref> and imaginal extinction.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Agren |first1=Thomas |last2=Björkstrand |first2=Johannes |last3=Fredrikson |first3=Mats |s2cid=25659581 |title=Disruption of human fear reconsolidation using imaginal and in vivo extinction |journal=Behavioural Brain Research |date=February 2017 |volume=319 |pages=9–15 |doi=10.1016/j.bbr.2016.11.014 |pmid=27840245 }}</ref> The retrieval-extinction procedure was also effective in clinical populations, including heroine addicts,<ref name="pmid22499948" /> tobacco smokers,<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Germeroth |first1=Lisa J. |last2=Carpenter |first2=Matthew J. |last3=Baker |first3=Nathaniel L. |last4=Froeliger |first4=Brett |last5=LaRowe |first5=Steven D. |last6=Saladin |first6=Michael E. |title=Effect of a Brief Memory Updating Intervention on Smoking Behavior |journal=JAMA Psychiatry |date=1 March 2017 |volume=74 |issue=3 |pages=214–223 |doi=10.1001/jamapsychiatry.2016.3148 |pmid=28146243 |pmc=5930385 |doi-access=free }}</ref> PTSD<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Vermes |first1=Joana Singer |last2=Ayres |first2=Ricardo |last3=Goés |first3=Adara Saito |last4=Real |first4=Natalia Del |last5=Araújo |first5=Álvaro Cabral |last6=Schiller |first6=Daniela |last7=Neto |first7=Francisco Lotufo |last8=Corchs with long-lasting effects.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Björkstrand |first1=Johannes |last2=Agren |first2=Thomas |last3=Åhs |first3=Fredrik |last4=Frick |first4=Andreas |last5=Larsson |first5=Elna-Marie |last6=Hjorth |first6=Olof |last7=Furmark |first7=Tomas |last8=Fredrikson |first8=Mats |s2cid=205894307 |title=Think twice, it's all right: Long lasting effects of disrupted reconsolidation on brain and behavior in human long-term fear |journal=Behavioural Brain Research |date=May 2017 |volume=324 |pages=125–129 |doi=10.1016/j.bbr.2017.02.016 |pmid=28214541 }}</ref> Some forms of therapy, such as [[coherence therapy]], are built on the principles of memory reconsolidation and are designed to maximally optimize this process.<ref>Lane, R.D. and Nadel, L. (2020) Neuroscience of Enduring Change: Implications for Psychotherapy, Oxford University Press, USA{{page needed|date=September 2020}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |last1=Ecker |first1=Bruce interference.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Gräff |first1=Johannes |last2=Joseph |first2=Nadine F. |last3=Horn |first3=Meryl E. |last4=Samiei |first4=Alireza |last5=Meng |first5=Jia |last6=Seo |doi-access=free }}</ref> Additional conceptual replications and demonstrations of reconsolidation updating using other forms of behavioral and non-invasive interferences have been reported.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Borgomaneri |first1=Sara |last2=Battaglia |first2=Simone |last3=Garofalo |first3=Sara |last4=Tortora |first4=Francesco |last5=Avenanti |first5=Alessio |last6=di retrieval-extinction effects and disputed the results.<ref>{{Cite journal |pmid = 20884753|year = 2010|last1 = Chan|first1 = W. prior to extinction learning |journal=Cortex |date=June 2016 |volume=79 |pages=112–122 |doi=10.1016/j.cortex.2016.03.015 |pmid=27111105 |doi-access=free }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |last1=Kindt postretrieval-extinction learning on return of contextually controlled cued fear. |journal=Behavioral Neuroscience |date=2014 |volume=128 |issue=4 |pages=474–481 |doi=10.1037/a0036688 |pmid=24841740 }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |last1=Zimmermann |first1=Josua |last2=Bach |first2=Dominik R. |title=Impact of a reminder/extinction procedure on threat-conditioned pupil size and skin conductance responses |journal=Learning & Memory |date=16 March 2020 |volume=27 |issue=4 |pages=164–172 |doi=10.1101/lm.050211.119 |pmid=32179658 |pmc=7079572 }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |last1=Warren |first1=Victor Taylor |last2=Anderson |first2=Kemp M. |last3=Kwon |first3=Cliffe |last4=Bosshardt |first4=Lauren |last5=Jovanovic |first5=Tanja |last6=Bradley |first6=Bekh |last7=Norrholm |first7=Seth |date=September 2014 |volume=113 |pages=165–173 |doi=10.1016/j.nlm.2013.10.014 |pmid=24183839 |pmc=4351258 }}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal |pmid = 28765085|year = 2017|last1 = Luyten|first1 = L.|last2 = Beckers|first2 = T.|title = A preregistered, direct replication attempt of the retrieval-extinction effect in cued fear conditioning in rats|journal = Neurobiology of Learning and Memory|volume = 144|pages = 208–215|doi = 10.1016/j.nlm.2017.07.014|pmc = 5931313}}</ref> Theoretical formulations<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Gershman |first1=Samuel J |last2=Monfils |first2=Marie-H |last3=Norman |first3=Kenneth A |last4=Niv |first4=Yael |title=The computational nature of memory modification |journal=eLife |date=15 March 2017 |volume=6 |article-number=e23763 |doi=10.7554/eLife.23763 |pmid=28294944 |pmc=5391211 |doi-access=free }}</ref> and empirical work suggest that inconsistencies in reconsolidation effects may depend on the degree of memory destabilization, as not every memory recall involves neural destabilization; or on the efficacy of the interference, which could differ across individuals and populations.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Cahill |first1=Emma N. |last2=Wood |first2=Melissa A. |last3=Everitt |first3=Barry J. |last4=Milton |first4=Amy L. |title=The role of prediction error and memory destabilization in extinction of cued-fear within the reconsolidation window |journal=Neuropsychopharmacology |date=20 December 2018 |volume=44 |issue=10 |pages=1762–1768 |doi=10.1038/s41386-018-0299-y |pmid=30659275 |pmc=6699995 }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |last1=Cahill |first1=Emma N. |last2=Milton |first2=Amy L. |title=Neurochemical and molecular mechanisms underlying the retrieval-extinction effect |journal=Psychopharmacology |date=17 January 2019 |volume=236 |issue=1 |pages=111–132 |doi=10.1007/s00213-018-5121-3 |pmid=30656364 |pmc=6373198 }}</ref><ref name="pmid27885549" /><ref>{{cite journal |last1=Cassini |first1=Lindsey F. |last2=Flavell |first2=Charlotte R. |last3=Amaral |first3=Olavo B. |last4=Lee |first4=Jonathan L.C. |title=On the transition from reconsolidation to extinction of contextual fear memories |journal=Learning & Memory |date=16 August 2017 |volume=24 |issue=9 |pages=392–399 |doi=10.1101/lm.045724.117 |pmid=28814464 |pmc=5580521 }}</ref><ref name="Reminder duration determines threat" />

===Research on imagination===
Using real-time fMRI, Schiller's research demonstrated that external motivational cues interact with neural substrates of [[motor imagery]]. The study also showed that neural regions that mediate motor imagery were synchronized with motor regions that produce actions.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Mendelsohn |first1=Avi |last2=Pine |first2=Alex |last3=Schiller |first3=Daniela |title=Between Thoughts and Actions: Motivationally Salient Cues Invigorate Mental Action in the Human Brain |journal=Neuron |date=January 2014 |volume=81 |issue=1 |pages=207–217 |doi=10.1016/j.neuron.2013.10.019 |pmid=24333054 |doi-access=free }}{{psc|date=July 2020}}</ref> Another study extracted the whole brain signature of learned fear and demonstrate that fear responses could be extinguished by imagining of the conditioned stimuli. Imagined extinction engaged brain regions that were also recruited by actual extinction, including the amygdala and the ventromedial prefrontal cortex. Neural activity in the [[nucleus accumbens]] predicted the ability to successfully extinguish fear by using imagination.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Reddan |first1=Marianne Cumella |last2=Wager |first2=Tor Dessart |last3=Schiller |first3=Daniela |title=Attenuating Neural Threat Expression with Imagination |journal=Neuron |date=November 2018 |volume=100 |issue=4 |pages=994–1005.e4 |doi=10.1016/j.neuron.2018.10.047 |pmid=30465766 |pmc=6314478 |doi-access=free }}{{psc|date=July 2020}}</ref>

===Research on social navigation===
The goal of this line of research is to uncover the neural representation of social relationships. Schiller's research have shown that forming first impressions recruits brain regions involved in emotion and valuation processes, including the amygdala and posterior cingulate cortex. Neural responses in these regions during an initial social encounter, predict subsequent impressions. This suggests that the attribution of social value to people and to things relies on similar basic neural mechanism rather than specialized neural circuits.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Schiller |first1=Daniela |last2=Freeman |first2=Jonathan B |last3=Mitchell |first3=Jason P |last4=Uleman |first4=James S |last5=Phelps |first5=Elizabeth A |s2cid=546583 |title=A neural mechanism of first impressions |journal=Nature Neuroscience |date=8 March 2009 |volume=12 |issue=4 |pages=508–514 |doi=10.1038/nn.2278 |pmid=19270690 }}</ref> Another line of research examines how the brain tracks dynamic social structure as people interact with others. To address this, Schiller's team created a social game in which participants arrive to an imaginary town and need to find a job and a place to live by interacting with the town's people. The study found that the location of each character relative to the participant in each interaction could be described by polar coordinates in a two-dimensional axis system of power and affiliation, and that these coordinates were encoded throughout the game by the hippocampus and the posterior cingulate cortex.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Tavares |first1=Rita Morais |last2=Mendelsohn |first2=Avi |last3=Grossman |first3=Yael |last4=Williams |first4=Christian Hamilton |last5=Shapiro |first5=Matthew |last6=Trope |first6=Yaacov |last7=Schiller |first7=Daniela |title=A Map for Social Navigation in the Human Brain |journal=Neuron |date=July 2015 |volume=87 |issue=1 |pages=231–243 |doi=10.1016/j.neuron.2015.06.011 |pmid=26139376 |pmc=4662863 |doi-access=free }}{{psc|date=July 2020}}</ref> The findings helped merging the divergent views of hippocampal function as a spatial navigation system versus a hub of episodic memories, and instead support the notion that the hippocampus represents a host of cognitive maps on various domains of experiences and across multiple dimensions.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Schiller |first1=D. |last2=Eichenbaum |first2=H. |last3=Buffalo |first3=E. A. |last4=Davachi |first4=L. |last5=Foster |first5=D. J. |last6=Leutgeb |first6=S. |last7=Ranganath |first7=C. |title=Memory and Space: Towards an Understanding of the Cognitive Map |journal=Journal of Neuroscience |date=14 October 2015 |volume=35 |issue=41 |pages=13904–13911 |doi=10.1523/JNEUROSCI.2618-15.2015 |pmid=26468191 |pmc=6608181 |doi-access=free }}{{psc|date=July 2020}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |last1=Schafer |first1=Matthew |last2=Schiller |first2=Daniela |title=Navigating Social Space |journal=Neuron |date=October 2018 |volume=100 |issue=2 |pages=476–489 |doi=10.1016/j.neuron.2018.10.006 |pmid=30359610 |pmc=6226014 |doi-access=free }}{{psc|date=July 2020}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web | url=https://www.scientificamerican.com/article/in-search-of-the-brains-social-road-maps/ | title=In Search of the Brain's Social Road Maps | website=[[Scientific American]] | date=February 2020 }}</ref>

==Select publications==

* {{cite journal |last1=Collins |first1=Katherine A. |last2=Mendelsohn |first2=Avi |last3=Cain |first3=Christopher K. |last4=Schiller |first4=Daniela |title=Taking Action in the Face of Threat: Neural Synchronization Predicts Adaptive Coping |journal=The Journal of Neuroscience |date=29 October 2014 |volume=34 |issue=44 |pages=14733–14738 |doi=10.1523/JNEUROSCI.2152-14.2014 |pmid=25355225 |pmc=4212070 }}
